title: Knowledge Check: Psychedelics
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 19: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this is a history, policy, and science class, not a how-to. Psychedelics carry real psychological risks, are unsafe for some people, and the clinical research happens under careful medical supervision. If you or someone you know is struggling, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, who owns sacred knowledge and what prohibition really costs, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What happened to psilocybin in 1970, and what is happening with it today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"It was placed in Schedule I as having no medical use, yet it is now in advanced trials for depression"},{"id":"q1b","text":"It was approved as a vitamin"},{"id":"q1c","text":"It was never studied"},{"id":"q1d","text":"It was made fully legal everywhere"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Psilocybin was classified as having no accepted medical use in 1970, halting research, yet the same compound is now in advanced trials for treatment-resistant depression. The plant did not change; the science and politics did.","citation":"Lesson: The Mushroom's Message (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Which psychedelic belongs specifically to the Amazon basin?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Ayahuasca, a brew of two Amazon plants used in healing ceremony"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Peyote"},{"id":"q2c","text":"San Pedro"},{"id":"q2d","text":"LSD"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Ayahuasca is a brew combining two Amazon plants, specific to the hot, humid lowland rainforest of Ecuador, Peru, Colombia, and Brazil.","citation":"Lesson: The Living Geography"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Why is geography in this episode described as also being belonging?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Each psychedelic is tied to a specific landscape and the people of that landscape"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Because they all grow in the same place"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Because no one uses them"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Because they have no cultural meaning"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Psilocybin, ayahuasca, peyote, and San Pedro each belong to a specific place and culture, so the plant, the land, and the people are bound together.","citation":"Lesson: The Living Geography"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"Why is peyote endangered?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"It takes 10 to 15 years to mature, grows only in the small Chihuahuan Desert, and is overharvested"},{"id":"q4b","text":"It grows everywhere and is overproduced"},{"id":"q4c","text":"It has no value"},{"id":"q4d","text":"It matures in a week"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Peyote is native only to a narrow desert zone and takes 10 to 15 years to mature, so overharvesting and habitat loss have left some wild populations depleted.","citation":"Lesson: Peyote's Endangered Geography"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What is the legal paradox around peyote?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Ceremonial use is protected for Native American Church members, but cultivation to supply it is prohibited, creating scarcity"},{"id":"q5b","text":"It is fully legal to farm and sell"},{"id":"q5c","text":"It is banned for everyone"},{"id":"q5d","text":"There are no laws about it"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"U.S. law allows ceremonial peyote use by Native American Church members but prohibits commercial cultivation, so the law protects the practice while starving it of the plant.","citation":"Lesson: Peyote's Endangered Geography"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"How does Indiana's prohibition affect residents who might benefit from clinical psychedelic therapy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"They cannot access it locally and must enroll in a clinical trial in another state"},{"id":"q6b","text":"They get free treatment"},{"id":"q6c","text":"There is no effect"},{"id":"q6d","text":"They can buy it anywhere"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Because Indiana keeps full prohibition, residents seeking supervised psilocybin therapy must travel to trials in states like Oregon, creating unequal therapeutic access.","citation":"Lesson: The New Map of Access"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What did Oregon do in 2020?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Created the first regulated psilocybin services, with licensed centers and a required trained guide"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Banned all psychedelics"},{"id":"q7c","text":"Allowed unsupervised home use"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Did nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Oregon became the first state to create regulated psilocybin services in 2020, with licensed service centers and a trained guide present, and no home use.","citation":"Lesson: The New Map of Access"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What common pattern runs across the four Indigenous psychedelic traditions?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Structured ceremony, a trained guide, community accountability, and the substance treated as sacred, not a casual chemical"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Open, casual recreational use"},{"id":"q8c","text":"Commercial sale to tourists"},{"id":"q8d","text":"No rules at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"All four traditions involve structured ceremony, a trained practitioner, community accountability, and treating the substance as a living sacred entity, never casual use.","citation":"Lesson: The Four Traditions"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What is the velada?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"The Mazatec all-night healing ceremony using psilocybin mushrooms"},{"id":"q9b","text":"A type of cactus"},{"id":"q9c","text":"A pharmaceutical company"},{"id":"q9d","text":"A U.S. law"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"The velada is the Mazatec all-night healing ceremony, guided by a healer, in which psilocybin mushrooms (teonanacatl) are used as sacred medicine.","citation":"Lesson: The Four Traditions"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What happened after Wasson published 'Seeking the Magic Mushroom' in 1957?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Seekers flooded Maria Sabina's community without consent, her home was burned, and she said the mushrooms lost their power"},{"id":"q10b","text":"The community was paid generously"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Nothing changed"},{"id":"q10d","text":"The mushrooms went extinct"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The Life Magazine article brought outsiders worldwide to Huautla de Jimenez, disrupting the community, and Maria Sabina later said the mushrooms lost their power after they came.","citation":"Lesson: Maria Sabina"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What does FPIC stand for, and what question does Maria Sabina's case raise about it?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Free, Prior, and Informed Consent; whether one community member can consent on behalf of a whole community"},{"id":"q11b","text":"A pharmaceutical patent"},{"id":"q11c","text":"A type of mushroom"},{"id":"q11d","text":"A research grant"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"FPIC is Free, Prior, and Informed Consent, and Maria Sabina's individual permission raised the question of whether one person can consent to cultural extraction for an entire community.","citation":"Lesson: Maria Sabina"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Why were psychedelics placed in Schedule I in 1970?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"A political move tied to targeting the counterculture, despite earlier clinical evidence of promise"},{"id":"q12b","text":"Because all research proved them worthless"},{"id":"q12c","text":"Because doctors requested it"},{"id":"q12d","text":"Because they were newly discovered"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Despite 1950s and 1960s research showing promise, psychedelics were made Schedule I in a political move connected to the counterculture, halting research for about thirty years.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacrament to Schedule I"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"When did psilocybin research revive in the United States?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"In 2006, when Johns Hopkins received the first FDA psilocybin research approval in over 30 years"},{"id":"q13b","text":"It never revived"},{"id":"q13c","text":"In 1970"},{"id":"q13d","text":"In 1938"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"After roughly thirty years of halted research, Johns Hopkins received the first FDA approval to study psilocybin again in 2006, beginning the modern revival.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacrament to Schedule I"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"How large is the projected psychedelic therapy market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"More than 10 billion dollars by 2030"},{"id":"q14b","text":"About 100 dollars"},{"id":"q14c","text":"There is no market"},{"id":"q14d","text":"About 1,000 dollars"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Analysts project a psychedelic therapy market of more than 10 billion dollars by 2030, with hundreds of millions already invested in trials.","citation":"Lesson: The Ten-Billion-Dollar Question"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What medical need is driving psychedelic therapy research?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Depression affects about 280 million people, and roughly 84 million have treatment-resistant depression"},{"id":"q15b","text":"There is no medical need"},{"id":"q15c","text":"Only mild headaches"},{"id":"q15d","text":"A shortage of vitamins"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"About 280 million people have depression, and roughly 84 million of them do not respond to standard antidepressants, the main target for psilocybin therapy.","citation":"Lesson: The Ten-Billion-Dollar Question"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What is biopiracy in the psychedelic context?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Building a profitable industry on Indigenous knowledge without consent or benefit-sharing, as with Mazatec mushroom knowledge"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Pirating movies about drugs"},{"id":"q16c","text":"A farming method"},{"id":"q16d","text":"A type of cactus"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Biopiracy is the commercial use of biological resources and traditional knowledge without consent or benefit-sharing, as the projected market builds on Mazatec knowledge with no royalties to the community.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is a key limitation of the 2010 Nagoya Protocol?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"It applies mainly to genetic resources, does not require acknowledging traditional-knowledge provenance, and has weak enforcement"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It bans all research"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It has no limitations"},{"id":"q17d","text":"It guarantees full benefit-sharing"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"The Nagoya Protocol aimed at fair benefit-sharing but applies mainly to genetic resources, does not require companies to credit traditional knowledge, and is weakly enforced.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"How does Oregon's service-center model differ from a pharmaceutical model?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"It keeps value in skilled guided service rather than an extracted, patented molecule, fitting Indigenous views better"},{"id":"q18b","text":"It patents the mushrooms"},{"id":"q18c","text":"It bans guides"},{"id":"q18d","text":"It is identical to the pharmaceutical model"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"The Oregon model treats the work as a guided service rather than a product to extract and patent, which is more compatible with Indigenous views of the knowledge as a practice.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What is the hidden cost of prohibiting psychedelics for thirty years?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Treatments that might have helped conditions like treatment-resistant depression were never developed, a foregone-healthcare cost"},{"id":"q19b","text":"There was no cost"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It saved billions"},{"id":"q19d","text":"It cured depression"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"The 1970 to 2006 research halt meant possible treatments were not developed, an invisible cost precisely because it consists of things that never happened.","citation":"Lesson: Prohibition's Hidden Cost"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does Oregon's regulated model compare economically to Schedule I prohibition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"It eliminates criminal penalties for regulated use, is expected to raise tax revenue, and builds a service economy"},{"id":"q20b","text":"It raises no revenue"},{"id":"q20c","text":"It costs the same as prohibition"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It bans all economic activity"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Where prohibition generated criminal-justice costs and zero tax revenue, Oregon's model removes penalties for regulated use, raises tax revenue, and supports licensed guides.","citation":"Lesson: Prohibition's Hidden Cost"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"Why is writing about altered states a special challenge?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"These states resist ordinary language, so writers must choose among strategies, each with a payoff and a risk"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Because it is illegal to write about them"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Because there is nothing to describe"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Because everyone has had them"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Altered consciousness resists everyday words, so writers reach for philosophical frameworks, sensory catalogs, analogy, or negative definition, each effective in some ways and risky in others.","citation":"Lesson: Writing the Indescribable"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What does the word 'entheogen' center?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"The ceremonial and sacred context, meaning 'that which generates the divine within'"},{"id":"q22b","text":"The recreational party scene"},{"id":"q22c","text":"A chemical formula"},{"id":"q22d","text":"A brand name"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"The term entheogen, meaning that which generates the divine within, was proposed to center the sacred and ceremonial context rather than the counterculture associations of 'psychedelic.'","citation":"Lesson: Writing the Indescribable"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What are the conventions of a peer-reviewed research paper?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"A stated sample size, a measured effect size, explicit limitations, and hedged conclusions, reviewed by experts"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Strong claims and a catchy hook"},{"id":"q23c","text":"No data at all"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Only opinions"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Peer-reviewed papers report sample and effect sizes, state limitations, and hedge conclusions, and are evaluated by other experts before publication.","citation":"Lesson: Peer Review Versus Press Release"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What does a science press release tend to do, compared to the journal article?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Use accessible language and stronger claims with a hook, which can overstate the careful findings"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Be harder to read than the paper"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Contain more limitations"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Be identical to the paper"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"A press release is built to be readable and to grab a general audience, so it tends to make stronger claims than the hedged, peer-reviewed paper supports.","citation":"Lesson: Peer Review Versus Press Release"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What question does Maria Sabina's chants raise as oral literature?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"What is lost in translation and who owns knowledge transmitted within a community through performance"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Whether to print them in color"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Their word count"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Oral literature lives in performance and community context, so publishing and translating it raises questions of what is lost and who owns it.","citation":"Lesson: Peer Review Versus Press Release"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What risk does this episode emphasize about psychedelics?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"They carry real psychological risks, are unsafe for some people, and clinical research happens under medical supervision"},{"id":"q26b","text":"They are completely safe for everyone"},{"id":"q26c","text":"They have no effects"},{"id":"q26d","text":"They are a food group"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"The episode is honest that psychedelics can cause intense distress, are unsafe with certain conditions or medications, and are studied under careful medical supervision, never as a do-it-yourself activity.","citation":"Lesson: The Mushroom's Message (intro)"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"Where do psilocybin mushrooms grow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Worldwide, with the most diversity in subtropical Mexico, where the Mazatec call them teonanacatl"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Only in Antarctica"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Only in laboratories"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Only underwater"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"There are more than 200 species growing on every inhabited continent, with the greatest diversity in subtropical Mexico, home of the Mazatec velada.","citation":"Lesson: The Living Geography"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"How does the Native American Church's peyote protection function?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"As a religious and civil-rights protection for ceremonial use, not a pharmacological judgment"},{"id":"q28b","text":"As a finding that peyote is safe for all"},{"id":"q28c","text":"As a commercial license"},{"id":"q28d","text":"As a ban"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Peyote's legal protection for the Native American Church is a religious-freedom and civil-rights achievement for ceremony, not a scientific ruling about the substance.","citation":"Lesson: The Four Traditions"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"Who synthesized LSD, and when?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Albert Hofmann at Sandoz, in 1938"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Timothy Leary, in 1968"},{"id":"q29c","text":"The Mazatec, in 3400 BCE"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Johns Hopkins, in 2006"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Albert Hofmann first synthesized LSD at the Sandoz laboratory in 1938, decades before it became associated with the counterculture.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacrament to Schedule I"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is the central idea of the psychedelics episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"These substances moved in both directions, criminalized as worthless then revived as medicine, while remaining sacred to the communities who knew them first"},{"id":"q30b","text":"They have no history"},{"id":"q30c","text":"They were invented by corporations"},{"id":"q30d","text":"They are simple recreational drugs"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Psychedelics are the clearest case of a substance both banned as worthless and revived as medicine, while staying sacred to living communities, three threads at once.","citation":"Lesson: The Mushroom's Message (intro)"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What philosophical metaphor did Aldous Huxley use in The Doors of Perception?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"A 'reducing valve' for the mind, which can over-intellectualize the experience"},{"id":"q31b","text":"A simple checklist"},{"id":"q31c","text":"A weather report"},{"id":"q31d","text":"A grocery list"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Huxley framed the mind as a reducing valve that normally filters perception, a philosophical framework that, the lesson notes, risks over-intellectualizing direct experience.","citation":"Lesson: Writing the Indescribable"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"How does ayahuasca tourism appear in the economics?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"It generates more than 70 million dollars a year in Peru, an industry already built on these plants"},{"id":"q32b","text":"It earns nothing"},{"id":"q32c","text":"It is illegal in Peru"},{"id":"q32d","text":"It does not exist"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Ayahuasca tourism brings more than 70 million dollars a year to Peru, showing an economy already forming around these sacred plants and raising the question of who benefits.","citation":"Lesson: The Ten-Billion-Dollar Question"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What does Michael Pollan's How to Change Your Mind do as science journalism?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"It bridges clinical research and narrative so the evidence carries the argument for a general audience"},{"id":"q33b","text":"It ignores the research"},{"id":"q33c","text":"It is a peer-reviewed study"},{"id":"q33d","text":"It is a press release"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Pollan alternates clinical research with narrative, translating peer-reviewed findings for a general audience while letting the evidence drive the argument.","citation":"Lesson: Peer Review Versus Press Release"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"How does the new legal geography of psilocybin mirror healthcare more broadly?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Access to treatment depends on where you live, not just on your diagnosis"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Everyone has identical access"},{"id":"q34c","text":"No one can access treatment"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Only doctors can travel"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Just as in healthcare overall, where you live can decide whether a promising treatment is available locally or only across the country, a geography of access.","citation":"Lesson: The New Map of Access"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"How were these substances treated within their Indigenous traditions?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"As living sacred entities used in community ceremony, never as casual recreational drugs"},{"id":"q35b","text":"As ordinary snacks"},{"id":"q35c","text":"As industrial chemicals"},{"id":"q35d","text":"As party drugs"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"In all four traditions the substances were sacred entities used within structured, guided, accountable community ceremony, not casual or recreational.","citation":"Lesson: The Four Traditions"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"If you or someone you know is struggling, what resource does this episode give?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"The SAMHSA helpline, free and confidential 24/7 at 1-800-662-4357"},{"id":"q36b","text":"No resources exist"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Only a retreat center"},{"id":"q36d","text":"A pharmaceutical hotline"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Every Season 3 episode points to the SAMHSA helpline, 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours, since psychedelics carry real psychological risks.","citation":"Lesson: The Mushroom's Message (intro)"}]}
